elements.sent-2411-001: Inspired by the work of Ulrich \cite{ulrich and Huneke--Ulrich \cite{hunekeulrich1988 , we describe a pattern to show that the ideals of certain opposite embedded Schubert varieties (defined by this pattern) arise by taking residual intersections of two (geometrically linked) opposite Schubert varieties (which we called {\it Ulrich pairs in \cite{FTW20 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: This pattern is uniform for the ADE types.
elements.sent-2411-003: Some of the free resolutions of the Schubert varieties in question are important for the structure of finite free resolutions.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our proof is representation theoretical and uniform for our pattern, however it is possible to derive our results using case-by-case analysis and the aid of a computer.
elements.sent-2411-005: The theory of linkage and residual intersections has arisen in a natural way in enumerative geometry, intersection theory, the study of Rees rings and integral closures.
elements.sent-2411-006: Linkage has been extensively studied (see \cite{watanabe, rao, ulrich, HU, ulrich2023remarks and the references therein); in particular it has given rise to the study of licci ideals, that is, the ideals which are in the linkage class of a complete intersection.}
elements.sent-2411-007: On the other hand, residual intersections \cite{artin1972residual, cumming2007residual,hunekeulrich1988,kustin1992generating, HU, fulton1984excess , have remained more obscure, although they have been more extensively studied in recent years \cite{ chardin2001hilbert,corso2002core,bruns1990resolution, boucca2019residual, hassanzadeh2016residual, hassanzadeh2024deformation .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: In this paper we construct a family of examples of residual intersections using the defining ideals of certain opposite Schubert varieties defined by a pattern that we describe below.
elements.sent-2411-009: The opposite Schubert varieties in question have been subject of recent investigations \cite{samweyman2021schubert, FTW20 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Let $D$ be a simply-laced Dynkin diagram and let $k$ be an extremal or minuscule vertex.
elements.sent-2411-011: We consider a rooted graph $\mathcal{G _{k}$ defined by attaching an extra distinguished vertex to $k$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This graph has a $``Y"$ shape where we display the distinguished vertex at the top, and the edges of $G_k$ correspond to the vertices of $D$.
elements.sent-2411-014: Let $G$ be a simply connected, reductive, complex algebraic group with Lie algebra associated to $D$.

elements.sent-2411-034: In Section 2 we collect basics on commutative algebra, and in Section 3 we collect basics on Schubert varieties.

elements.sent-2411-043: An ideal $I$ is {\it perfect if its grade coincides with its projective dimension $\mathrm{grade (I) = \mathrm{pd _R(R/I)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: We say that $I$ is {\it Gorenstein if $I$ is perfect and $t(I)=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The height $\mathrm{ht (I)$ of $I$ is the minimum of the heights of the prime ideals in $R$ that contain it.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The height of a prime ideal is simply the codimension of the variety defined by it.
elements.sent-2411-047: A proper ideal $I$ in $R$ is called unmixed if $\mathrm{ht (P) = \mathrm{ht (I)$ for every associated prime $P$ of $R/I$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: As $R$ is Cohen-Macaulay, we have that $\mathrm{grade (I) = \mathrm{ht (I)$ for any proper ideal $I$, and by slight abuse of terminology we will often call this the codimension of $I$.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The colon ideal or ideal quotient is $I:J := \left\{r \in R | rJ \subset I \right\}$.
elements.sent-2411-051: The ideals $I,J$ in R are said to be {\it linked if they are both proper and if there exists a regular sequence $a= \{a_1, \ldots, a_g\}$ in $R$ such that $J = (a):I$ and $I = (a):J$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: If $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay and $I, J$ are linked, then $I$ and $J$ are unmixed of the same height $g$.
elements.sent-2411-053: A link is called geometric if $\mathrm{ht (I+J) \geq g+1$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Moreover, if (\ref{geometriclinkage ) holds, $I$ and $J$ are both unmixed of the same height and have no common prime components, then they are (geometrically) linked.}

elements.sent-2411-274: For $i = 1$: \begin{equation}\operatorname{res}^{g}_{g^{i}} ( V(\omega_{2}) ) = {\color{blue} V(\omega_{2})} \oplus {\color{red}V(\omega_{5})} \oplus {\color{orange} V(\omega_{2}) \oplus V(\omega_{5}) \oplus V(\omega_{2})} \oplus {\color{blue} V(\omega_{7})} \oplus { \color{orange} V(\omega_{3}+ \omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{7})}\end{equation} There are thirty-two extremal Pl\"ucker coordinates dual to $V(\omega_{2 )$ which generate $X^{1}$ and twelve more non-extremal ones dual to $V(\omega_{7 )$.}}
elements.formula-2411-041: \begin{equation}\operatorname{res}^{g}_{g^{i}} ( V(\omega_{2}) ) = {\color{blue} V(\omega_{2})} \oplus {\color{red}V(\omega_{5})} \oplus {\color{orange} V(\omega_{2}) \oplus V(\omega_{5}) \oplus V(\omega_{2})} \oplus {\color{blue} V(\omega_{7})} \oplus { \color{orange} V(\omega_{3}+ \omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{7})}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-275: For $i = 6$: \begin{equation}\operatorname{res}^{g}_{g^{i}} ( V(\omega_{2}) ) = {\color{blue} V(\omega_{5})} \oplus {\color{red}V(\omega_{3} + \omega_{7})} \oplus V(\omega_{1}+ \omega_{5}) \oplus V(\omega_{1} + \omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{1} + \omega_{2}) \oplus V(\omega_{3} + \omega_{1}) \oplus V(\omega_{2}) \oplus {\color{blue}V(\omega_{7})} \oplus V(\omega_{2}) \oplus V(\omega_{4} + \omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{5}) \oplus V(\omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{2} + 2 \omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{1} + \omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{5} + 2 \omega_{1})\end{equation} There are sixteen extremal Pl\"ucker coordinates dual to ${ \color{blue V(\omega_{5 ) }$ vanishing on $X^{6}$ and two more non-extremal Pl\"ucker coordinates dual to ${\color{blue V(\omega_{7 )}$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-042: \begin{equation}\operatorname{res}^{g}_{g^{i}} ( V(\omega_{2}) ) = {\color{blue} V(\omega_{5})} \oplus {\color{red}V(\omega_{3} + \omega_{7})} \oplus V(\omega_{1}+ \omega_{5}) \oplus V(\omega_{1} + \omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{1} + \omega_{2}) \oplus V(\omega_{3} + \omega_{1}) \oplus V(\omega_{2}) \oplus {\color{blue}V(\omega_{7})} \oplus V(\omega_{2}) \oplus V(\omega_{4} + \omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{5}) \oplus V(\omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{2} + 2 \omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{1} + \omega_{7}) \oplus V(\omega_{5} + 2 \omega_{1})\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-276: For $i = 7$: \begin{equation}\operatorname{res}^{g}_{g^{i}} ( V(\omega_{2}) ) = {\color{blue} V(\omega_{2})} \oplus {\color{red}V(\omega_{5})} \oplus V(\omega_{3}) \oplus V(\omega_{2}) \oplus { \color{blue} V(\omega_{1}) } \oplus V(\omega_{6})\end{equation} Dual to ${\color{blue V(\omega_{2 )}$, there are seventy-two extremal Pl\"ucker coordinates as well as six non-extremal Pl\"ucker coordinates vanishing on $X^{7}$.}}
elements.formula-2411-043: \begin{equation}\operatorname{res}^{g}_{g^{i}} ( V(\omega_{2}) ) = {\color{blue} V(\omega_{2})} \oplus {\color{red}V(\omega_{5})} \oplus V(\omega_{3}) \oplus V(\omega_{2}) \oplus { \color{blue} V(\omega_{1}) } \oplus V(\omega_{6})\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-277: There are twenty-seven more non-extremal Pl\"ucker coordinates dual to ${\color{blue V(\omega_{1 )}$.}}
